prompt: Explain the water cycle, in prose, without using any lists, bullets, headings, or numbered steps. Keep it to one paragraph.
response: The water cycle is the endless, natural journey water takes as it moves through the Earth and its atmosphere, constantly shifting forms and locations. It begins when the sun’s warmth causes water from oceans, lakes, rivers, and even soil to rise into the air as vapor—a process called evaporation. Plants also contribute by releasing water vapor through their leaves in a quiet exhale known as transpiration. As this moist air ascends, it cools and condenses into tiny droplets, forming clouds that drift on the wind. When these droplets grow heavy enough, they fall back to the ground as precipitation—rain, snow, sleet, or hail—nourishing the land and replenishing rivers, lakes, and underground aquifers. Some of this water flows across the surface, carving paths toward larger bodies of water, while some seeps deep into the earth, feeding hidden reservoirs. Eventually, much of it returns to the oceans, where the cycle begins anew, a seamless loop that sustains life, shapes landscapes, and connects every corner of the planet in an invisible, ever-flowing dance.
